consolidated.stmt: Opposing airlock boundary doors may open concurrently during a transfer, compromising airlock containment.
During a transfer, both zone-facing boundaries may remain open because the transfer load obstructs complete closure, compromising zone separation.

clusters.need: Reduce the percentage of credible cascade-breaching transitions excluded from acceptance evaluation

consolidated.stmt: Reduce the percentage of credible cascade-breaching transitions excluded from acceptance evaluation

Reduce the number of unresolved contradictions between throughput and boundary-protection requirements

clusters.need: Stakeholders need control and boundary behavior to indicate status accurately, reach defined safe states during faults, and be verified across all credible conditions.

consolidated.stmt: Reduce the number of defined fault scenarios in which control logic fails to reach the specified safe state

Reduce the number of credible fault states lacking defined boundary-safe behavior
Reduce the number of credible worst-case transfer states omitted from boundary qualification
Reduce the number of evaluated states in which reported control status conflicts with observed access or boundary state
Reduce the percentage of required operating states and transitions omitted from verification

clusters.need: Stakeholders need to recognize and classify boundary breaches as quickly as possible after they begin.

consolidated.stmt: Minimize the time between occurrence of a boundary breach and classification of the event as a breach
Reduce the duration between boundary-breach onset and operator recognition

clusters.need: Stakeholders need clear, correct pressure requirements that consistently preserve the intended hierarchy and contamination boundaries during and after transfers.

consolidated.stmt: A barrier may release before the required pressure differential is established or restored, compromising the pressure cascade.
Transfers use inappropriate pressure limits or lose required pressure separation, allowing prolonged differential excursions or airflow reversal between classified zones.
Pressure differential falls below the boundary-preserving threshold during transfer, weakening contamination control in proportion to the shortfall and its duration.
Minimize the likelihood that a transfer begins while baseline differential pressure is outside validated limits
Pressure requirements between adjacent classified zones are ambiguous or specified in the wrong direction, causing loss of the required hierarchy to go unrecognized as a boundary breach.
Reduce the frequency of pressure differentials falling outside specified limits during expected facility fluctuations
Reduce the number of adjacent-zone classifications or pressure baselines identified incorrectly in the pressure-cascade requirements
Reduce the frequency of differential pressure crossing validated limits during transfers
Minimize the likelihood that displayed pressure readings misrepresent actual zone differentials during transfers
Reduce the frequency of environmental differential excursions below the required classification limit at transfer points
Reduce the frequency of pressure-cascade excursions during material or personnel transfer
Reduce the degree of mismatch between verification conditions and defined transfer, occupancy, and zone-pressure conditions
Reduce the percentage of pressure alarms configured inconsistently with validated excursion limits
Pressure-differential requirements remain ambiguous about applicable reference locations and allowable values for defined transfer states, risking inconsistent interpretation and implementation.
Reduce the number of relevant idle, loading, unloading, and boundary-opening states omitted from the pressure requirements
Reduce the time the transfer interface remains outside the ready-state pressure range after transfer
Zone pressure differentials remain outside specified limits too long after all zone-facing barriers close, prolonging pressure-boundary excursions.
Reduce the frequency of pressure-control oscillation between adjacent zones during transfer transitions
Reduce the percentage of normal pressure fluctuations misclassified as breaches

clusters.need: Stakeholders need transfers to occur without obstruction, spillage, incomplete closure, or opposing open doors that could compromise containment and environmental control.

consolidated.stmt: Chamber conditions, obstructing loads, or unsecured doors allow transfer with incomplete closure or both opposing doors open, compromising containment.
Incorrectly selected, staged, or contained materials can obstruct airflow or door closure, or spill inside the chamber, compromising safe and effective operation.
Materials or personnel obstruct full airlock-door closure during transfers, increasing the risk of incomplete containment and compromised environmental control.

consolidated.stmt: Minimize the degree of residual contamination on prepared material exteriors before transfer
Minimize the percent of each item's required external surface area left untreated before transfer

clusters.need: Stakeholders need contamination hazards consistently prevented, removed, and objectively verified before personnel, equipment, or materials cross or contact classified boundaries.

consolidated.stmt: Personnel or material transfers carry residual contamination into a cleaner classified zone, compromising environmental control.
The chamber retains contamination, residue, materials, packaging, or debris after transfer, exposing materials or personnel during subsequent passage.

Contamination crosses or spreads beyond the chamber boundary through pressure changes, open or unintended pathways, cleaning, sanitization, or containment actions, contaminating surfaces or adjacent items.
Chamber boundary surfaces or internal regions remain outside adequate decontamination and acceptance coverage before integrity verification, risking incomplete contamination control.
Items are released without effective surface decontamination because contamination levels, treatment requirements, or contact periods fall outside qualified ranges for the item or destination zone.
Material packaging is damaged during passage or corrective handling, releasing contaminants into the controlled environment.
Transfer-contact surfaces retain or reacquire contamination, prior-transfer material, or cleaning residue before reuse, exposing prepared materials to contamination.
Prepared materials contact contaminated or non-designated chamber surfaces during transfer, risking recontamination and compromising cleanliness.
Prepared-material containers sustain sterile-barrier damage during chamber passage, risking loss of sterility and material usability.
Protective attire at classified-zone entry may be contaminated, damaged, incomplete, or poorly fitted, increasing contamination risk and exposing skin.

Decontamination acceptance criteria omit relevant contamination hazards, lack traceability to governing requirements, or lack objective pass-or-fail thresholds, risking inconsistent acceptance of inadequately decontaminated equipment.
